title: Best Picture Power Rankings and Wes Anderson’s Oscar Chances
format: Sean and Amanda size up an unusually starry Oscar short-film field: Wes Anderson's four Roald Dahl adaptations plus new shorts from Almodóvar, Reggio, and Kaufman, then cut their Best Picture contenders from 22 down to 10. Sean closes with Grant Singer on his debut feature, 'Reptile.'

segments.heading: The short-film field
segments.note: seven shorts and Wes Anderson's Oscar odds

films.title: The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar
films.note: The longest and most-watched of Anderson's four Roald Dahl shorts for Netflix; Amanda saw it in Venice, where Anderson took the Cartier Glory to the Filmmaker Award. Benedict Cumberbatch, Dev Patel, Ralph Fiennes, Richard Ayoade, Rupert Friend, and Ben Kingsley pass narration around at high speed. Sean and Amanda expect it nominated for Best Short, given the Netflix money behind it, and read the whole quartet as an extension of 'Asteroid City' and 'The French Dispatch': more picture frames around the picture.

films.title: The Swan
films.note: Sean's favorite of the four Dahl shorts.

films.title: Poison
films.note: Issued individually on Netflix rather than packaged. Its thumbnail is just a picture of Benedict Cumberbatch, with no 'Wes Anderson presents' framing.

films.title: The Rat Catcher
films.note: The one Amanda watched first; Netflix served it with no autoplay to the next short, which both hosts read as deliberate.

films.title: Strange Way of Life
films.note: Almodóvar's second English-language short, a roughly 25-minute gay Western with Ethan Hawke and Pedro Pascal, funded and costumed by Saint Laurent. Amanda loves his work but found this rote and half-baked, more a trial balloon for his first English-language feature. Sean agrees it moves fast and covers familiar ground.

films.title: The Human Voice
films.note: Almodóvar's first English-language short, a single Tilda Swinton performance on a classical soundstage set; screened with 'Strange Way of Life' and a Q&A. Amanda preferred it: it works as a thought experiment that couldn't sustain a feature.

films.title: Once Within a Time
films.note: Godfrey Reggio's first film in over a decade, again scored by Philip Glass and executive produced by Steven Soderbergh. A black-and-white-and-colorized anarchic comedy that homages Méliès and German Expressionism. Sean saw it in a theater and pointed listeners to 'Koyaanisqatsi'; Amanda watched at home and argued it belongs in a MoMA gallery context.

films.title: Jackals & Fireflies
films.note: Charlie Kaufman's 22-minute short built around the poetry of Eva H.D., shot on a Samsung S22 Ultra and paid for by Samsung, on YouTube. More impressionistic than Kaufman's usual linear work; the hosts found it hit-or-miss but a genuine experiment.

segments.heading: Best Picture Power Rankings
segments.note: 22 contenders cut to 10, only two of them actually released

films.title: American Fiction
films.note: In. Cord Jefferson's satirical dramedy; Sean liked it and calls it a contender but not a lock, since this genre rarely breaks through.

films.title: All of Us Strangers
films.note: In. Andrew Scott, Claire Foy, and Jamie Bell; Amanda was wrecked by it, a Searchlight release she expects to campaign hard.

films.title: Barbie
films.note: In. One of two contenders already released; the hosts argue it plays broadly enough to win over the Academy's boomers.

films.title: The Color Purple
films.note: In. Neither host has seen it (it doesn't open until Christmas week and skipped the festivals), but they hold a spot for it as the musical/period piece the field is missing. Same studio as 'Barbie,' a possible campaign conflict.

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: In. Alexander Payne's new one; Sean hasn't seen it yet but keeps a place for it.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: In. All but a lock: Sean can't picture a scenario where Scorsese misses. A longer conversation is coming October 20th.

films.title: Maestro
films.note: In. Bradley Cooper's Leonard Bernstein film; one of Sean's favorites of the year. Reception flipped from cool in Venice to strong at the New York Film Festival.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: In. Sean's firm frontrunner, the other already-released contender; academy-friendly and, per an Owen Gleiberman column, arguably strongest in its final hour.

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: In. A no-brainer for both; Emma Stone, whom Sean has long called the actress of her generation, is his Best Actress favorite.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: In. Among the best films Sean has seen this year; he swaps it in for 'Past Lives' as the A24 slot, despite Richard Brody's contrarian pan.

films.title: Anatomy of a Fall
films.note: Holdover. Justine Triet's Palme d'Or winner with Sandra Hüller, distributed by Neon; Sean wants it to make a move. Opens October 13th.

films.title: May December
films.note: Holdover. Todd Haynes's film with Julianne Moore, Natalie Portman, and Charles Melton; the hosts, who haven't seen it, guess all three leads get nominated while the film misses Best Picture.

films.title: Napoleon
films.note: Holdover. Ridley Scott's epic with Joaquin Phoenix and Vanessa Kirby, with a four-hour director's cut promised for Apple TV+; the hosts sense costume-epic fatigue and an unlucky year opposite 'Oppenheimer.'

films.title: Past Lives
films.note: Holdover. Greta Lee in Celine Song's beloved A24 debut; the hosts fear it gets chopped for 'The Zone of Interest' and worry her Oscar hopes are hurt by 'The Morning Show.'

films.title: Air
films.note: Out. Sean eliminates it outright; Amanda enjoyed it but wouldn't put it in her top ten.

films.title: Asteroid City
films.note: Out. Rising in Sean's estimation (he thinks they'll regret not celebrating it), but both agree it isn't contending.

films.title: The Bike Riders
films.note: Out. Jeff Nichols's new film; Sean likes it a lot and sees possible acting nominations, but not Best Picture in a crowded field.

films.title: Ferrari
films.note: Out. Michael Mann's return; Sean loved it (an 'A-plus experience') but doesn't think it's Academy material.

films.title: The Killer
films.note: Out. Fincher's hitman movie, from the filmmaker Sean says he shares spiritual DNA with, but he'd be stunned if the Academy nominated it.

films.title: Nyad
films.note: Out for Best Picture, alive in acting. Annette Bening and Jodie Foster carry it; some CGI swings don't land, but Sean and Amanda would watch these two do anything.

films.title: Priscilla
films.note: Out. Sofia Coppola's film, which Sean liked and will dig into later, but neither expects a nomination.

films.title: Saltburn
films.note: Out. Emerald Fennell's divisive follow-up to 'Promising Young Woman'; Sean reserves the right to be wrong but doesn't see it getting in.

films.title: Reptile
films.note: Singer's debut feature, a Netflix crime thriller with Benicio del Toro, Justin Timberlake, Alicia Silverstone, Eric Bogosian, and Michael Pitt. The music-video director (The Weeknd, Lorde, Sky Ferreira) describes building the film around a mid-story protagonist handoff, a deliberately restrained and classical style, and peripheral scenes, like the kitchen remodel and the square-dance hall, that shade the world. Cut out of order over a year with editor Kevin Hickman; an executive's note was 'less Lost Highway, more Chinatown.'

films.title: Sicario
films.note: How Singer got the meeting: his producers had made it with Benicio del Toro.

films.title: In Cold Blood
films.note: One of the crime films Singer names as inspiration.

films.title: The Night of the Hunter
films.note: Cited among his touchstones for the genre.

films.title: Serpico
films.note: Another of the older crime pictures Singer says shaped 'Reptile.'

films.title: The Conversation
films.note: Singer wanted to portray the hunter as the hunted, as Coppola does here.

films.title: Psycho
films.note: His model for the protagonist switch a third of the way in; Singer is a big Hitchcock fan.

films.title: Chinatown
films.note: The good end of the famous studio note: 'less Lost Highway, more Chinatown.'

films.title: Lost Highway
films.note: The Lynchian pole earlier drafts leaned toward; Singer notes it was made for a far smaller audience than a Netflix film reaches.

films.title: Vertigo
films.note: One of Singer's favorite films; he praises its rhythm and the way Bernard Herrmann's score is in dialogue with the images, and reads it as being about healing through the recreation of trauma.

films.title: You Were Never Really Here
films.note: Lynne Ramsay's film, which Singer calls phenomenal and holds up as an example of rhythmic, propulsive filmmaking.

films.title: Wild Strawberries
films.note: Singer's pick for the last great thing he's seen: Bergman rewatched after finishing 'Reptile,' arriving at a non-verbal emotional depth he finds staggering.

films.title: Shoeshine
films.note: Named among the dream-like films that changed his life.

films: The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou

The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar.title: The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar

The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou.title: The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou

The French Dispatch.title: The French Dispatch of the Liberty, Kansas Evening Sun
